errors.annotator_notes: Title: 'Phylogenetic network analysis of SARS-CoV-2 genomes'
Background on mitochondrial DNA phylogenetics, network methods, application to coronavirus evolution
Sequences taken from GISAID database, not collected by authors. No clinical specimen collection described
160 complete genomes used, from 253 available in GISAID as of March 4, 2020. Selection: most complete genomes
Network5011CS software package; median-joining network algorithm and Steiner tree algorithm; epsilon=0 and epsilon=10
Median-joining network (Bandelt et al. 1999) and Steiner algorithm (Polzin & Daneshmand 2003), 288 most-parsimonious trees of length 229
Parsimony analysis, recurrent mutations at np11083 noted; gap coding as single deletion events
160 human + 1 bat virus, 101 distinct genomic sequence types; range 56-29797; bat 96.2% similarity
Infection tracing (Brazil-Italy, Ontario-Guangdong, Mexico-Italy-Germany-Wuhan chains); founder effects vs immunological adaptation hypothesis
Network5011CS software package freely available as shareware; coronavirus network files downloadable
No competing interest declared; acknowledgments to GISAID contributors and labs
Chi-square test, Fisher exact test mentioned
No discussion of missing data handling
Interpretation given but one-sided, no caveats
Structured abstract with Objective, Methods, Results, Conclusion
Rationale: HCQ attention, lack of good evidence, need for systematic evaluation of RCTs
Objective: evaluate efficacy and safety of HCQ for COVID-19 patients (mortality, serious adverse events)
Full MEDLINE search strategy with MeSH terms and text words provided
Random-effects model, Mantel-Haenszel method, risk ratio with 95% CI
Cochran's Q statistic and I² for heterogeneity (I²>50% threshold)
Table 1: country, site, drug, HCQ regimens, control, age, sample sizes, diagnosis method per study
Figure 2: risk of bias assessment (summarized and individual per study)
Forest plot (Figure 4) with risk difference showing individual study estimates
Table 2: Summary of findings with GRADE assessment (low for mortality, very low for adverse events)
Summary: no significant mortality reduction with HCQ (RR 1.09, 95% CI 0.99-1.19), no increase in adverse events
Limitations: heterogeneous populations, different HCQ dose criteria, RECOVERY trial dominates weight, only 2 double-blinded
Conclusion: HCQ not efficacious in reducing mortality of COVID-19 patients
PROSPERO CRD42020197070 registered
Structured abstract with Background, Methods, Results, Conclusions
Introduction covers glucocorticoid rationale, inflammation in Covid-19, lack of evidence
Objective: evaluate effect of dexamethasone on 28-day mortality
Eligibility: hospitalized Covid-19, clinically suspected or lab-confirmed, no contraindication
Dexamethasone 6mg once daily oral/IV for up to 10 days vs usual care
Power calculation: 2000 dexa + 4000 control gives 90% power at P=0.01 to detect 20% reduction
Steering committee monitored data, closed recruitment June 8 2020 when >2000 enrolled
Web-based randomization system with concealment of group assignment
Unstratified randomization (mentioned: 'through the play of chance in the unstratified randomization')
Concealment via Web-based system described
Open-label: patients and local members of trial staff were aware of assignments
Prespecified subgroup analyses by age, sex, race, respiratory support, days since symptom onset
Figure 1: CONSORT flow diagram with enrollment, randomization, allocation, analysis
Reasons for exclusion: 1948 excluded (357 no drug, 1707 not suitable)
Enrollment March 19 to June 8, 2020
Trial stopped by steering committee when enrollment exceeded 2000 in dexa group
Table 1: detailed baseline characteristics by treatment group and respiratory support level
All analyses intention-to-treat: all 2104 dexa and 4321 usual care included
Age-adjusted absolute reductions reported: 12.3 percentage points for ventilated, 4.2 for oxygen
Subgroup analyses by respiratory support (Figure 3), sensitivity analyses without age adjustment (Table S3)
4 serious adverse reactions: 2 hyperglycemia, 1 GI hemorrhage, 1 psychosis
Extensive limitations: open-label, no physiologic/virologic measures, only essential data collected
Generalizability discussed: ~10% of UK hospitalized Covid-19 patients, mortality consistent with national data
Discussion of findings in context of prior evidence, cautious interpretation for non-oxygen patients
Protocol available at NEJM.org and www.recoverytrial.net
Objective: investigate children with enterocolitis and developmental regression
Royal Free Hospital, London. Children admitted for 1 week
12 consecutively referred children described with referral criteria
Not a case-control study
Outcomes: GI histology, neurological assessment, behavioral diagnosis
Mann-Whitney U test, chi-square test mentioned
Key finding: associated GI disease and developmental regression
Acknowledges: did not prove association, further investigation needed
Funding source not clearly stated in paper
